name: Manjistha
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Prominently pacifies Pitta and Kapha doshas. Due to its Tikta (bitter), Kashaya (astringent), and Madhura (sweet) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Raktaprasadana' (blood-soothing) and 'Varnya' (complexion-promoting) therapies, clearing the fiery and agitated qualities of Pitta and the heavy qualities of Kapha through its systemic detoxifying property. It may increase Vata if used in excess.

Karma (Actions): Raktaprasadana (blood-soothing), Varnya (complexion-promoting), Vishaghna (anti-toxic), Artavajanana (menstrual-restoring), Raktashodhaka (blood-purifier), and Jwarahara (antipyretic).

API/AFI Adherence: The dried root is the official medicinal part as per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is the central ingredient in almost all classical blood-purifying and skin-toning formulations like Manjisthadi Kwatha, Mahamanjisthadyarishta, and various face-masks (Lepas).
Specific Indications: Used in Kushtha (Skin diseases), Visarpa (Erysipelas), Vyanga (Pigmentation/Melasma), Raktapitta (Bleeding disorders), Artava-dosha (menstrual disorders), and Kamala (Jaundice).
usageForm: Root powder (Churna), Root decoction (Kwatha), Medicated oil (Taila), Fermented extract (Arishta).
mainMedicinalUses: World-Famous Blood Purifier Specialist (Raktashodhaka): Manjistha is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for all conditions of blood toxicity and skin diseases. Its 'Raktaprasadana' (blood-soothing) nature provided by anthraquinones helps in clearing deep-seated pathogens and metabolic toxins (Ama) from the blood stream. WHO-aligned research confirms its potent anti-oxidant, anti-tumor, and anti-inflammatory properties.

Skin Health and Complexion enhancement: Known as 'Varnya' (beneficial for skin). It effectively reduces skin discoloration, age-spots, and acne by providing systemic blood-purifying effects. The root paste applied topically promotes rapid relief from hyperpigmentation and inflammatory skin lesions.

Female Menstrual Support: Known as 'Artavajanana' (menstrual-restoring). It is traditionally used to manage irregular menstruation, painful periods, and uterine congestion by providing systemic hormonal balance and warming the pelvic region.

Liver and Spleen Detox: Acts as a powerful agent for managing liver and spleen health. It is extensively used to treat jaundice and fatty liver by promoting healthy lymphatic drainage and clearing congestion in the 'Yakṛt' (liver) system through its systemic blood-purifying action.

Urinary and Kidney Care: As a mild diuretic, it helps in clearing urinary stagnations, reducing burning sensation, and preventing the formation of kidney stones (Ashmari) by cooling the Pitta in the bladder and kidneys.

Cancer and Immune support: Traditionally used as 'Rasayana' (rejuvenative) for its anti-tumor and immune-modulating properties. It helps in protecting the body's 'Ojas' (vital essence) and promoting healthy cell regeneration by cleared metabolic toxins.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in Anthraquinones (Alizarin, Purpurin, Munjistin), Iridoids, Glycosides, and diversos Pentacyclic Triterpenes. Purpurin is the primary bioactive responsible for its potent anti-microbial, anti-tumor, and anti-inflammatory action on the blood and skin cells.
plantProfile: Unique History: Classified under the 'Varnya' (complexion-promoting) and 'Raktaprasadana' (blood-soothing) groups in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. The name 'Manjistha' literally translates to 'one that stays deep-red' (referring to the color of the roots and its affinity for blood). It is spiritually associated with 'Sattva' (purity and clarity). Mentioned in classical Sanskrit literature as the 'Indian Madder'.

Physical Characteristics: A perennial, prickly climbing or trailing herb growing up to 3-5 meters in height, with quadrangular stems and opposite, heart-shaped (cordate), leathery leaves. The flowers are small, greenish-white, in terminal and axillary cymes. The root is thick, long, cylindrical, brownish-red with a characteristic red-orange interior. It is found throughout India, particularly in the sub-Himalayan tract and deciduous forests.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Anthraquinones (Purpurin, Alizarin). The presence of purpurin is crucial for its neuro-relaxant, anti-tumor, and skin-regenerative effects (when appropriately diluted).

Strict Standardizations: The root is standardized in API based on its brownish-red color and characteristic astringent-bitter-sweet taste. Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<12%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%), and water-soluble extractive (>15%). HPLC is used to monitor 1% to 2% purpurin content in commercial extracts.
preparationMethods: Root Powder (Churna): 3-6g of fine powder taken twice daily with honey or warm water (as an Anupana) for managing blood and skin fevers.
Root Decoction (Kwatha): 20g of root boiled in 160ml water reduced to 40ml for chronic liver and skin issues. Take twice daily before meals.
Manjisthadi Taila: Medicated oil preparation used primarily for local application on the skin for complexion and inflammatory lesions.
Mahamanjisthadyarishta: 15-30ml of fermented extract taken twice daily after meals for managing chronic gut infections and piles through its blood-purifying action.
External Paste (Lepa): Ground fresh root or powder applied directly to localized inflammations (Mumps, boils, skin rashes, hyperpigmentation) to reduce heat and swelling.

dosages.Children: Generally safe. 125 mg to 250 mg of powder mixed with honey once or twice daily for digestive protection and skin relief during childhood infections.
dosages.Adults: Powder: 3-6 grams; Decoction: 20-50 ml; Arishta: 15-30 ml, divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Ideal for age-related Pitta issues and maintaining cardiac health. Dosage: 3g of powder daily with warm milk at bedtime.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Generally very safe. However, individuals with high gastric sensitivity, and during pregnancy (abortifacient risk in very large doses). Avoid in extreme exhaustion unless under professional guidance.

GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb and dye with a history of use spanning millennia across various global cultures and traditional systems (Ayurveda, Unani, and TCM) without documented toxicity.

Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-diabetic or anticoagulant medications as it may potentiate their effects. No severe adverse interactions reported in clinical literature.

Adverse Effects: None documented at traditional therapeutic levels. Excessive use in very sensitive individuals may rarely cause mild skin cooling or stomach dryness. May cause harmless reddish discoloration of urine and stools.
